index.title: Resource Translator
index.description: Resource Translator opens machine-translated pull requests for your resource files in any repo, in any format — powered by Azure AI Translator, AWS Translate, or Google Cloud Translation.
index.socialTitle: Resource Translator — translate resource files automatically
index.badge: v3.0 — Azure, AWS & Google providers, Node 24, repo config
index.lede: Resource Translator opens machine-translated pull requests for your resource files using Azure, AWS, or Google — one unified action, one provider per run, official SDKs under the hood.
index.workflowTitle: Translation as a workflow, not a chore.
index.workflowBody: Drop the action into your existing CI, point it at Azure, AWS, or Google, and let your repo speak every language you target.

config.title: Repo-level config
config.body: Drop a {configPath} file for include globs, glossaries, and Custom Translator categories.

glossary.title: Glossary-aware
glossary.body: Lock brand names and product terms with smart word-boundary regex that handles {cpp}, {dotnet}, and friends.

dryRun.title: Dry-run + soft-fail
dryRun.body: Preview translations without writing files, or surface every error as a warning while you iterate.

sdk.title: Azure, AWS & Google
sdk.body: Pick one provider per run. Each is driven by its official SDK ({azureSdk}, {awsSdk}, {googleSdk}) with automatic retries on {statusCodes} — no hand-rolled HTTP. {esbuild}-bundled, audit-clean.

workflowPreview.body: Three secrets, four lines, and your repo is multilingual on the next push.

cta.title: Ready to localize on autopilot?
cta.body: One provider key — Azure, AWS, or Google — and one workflow file. Resource Translator handles the rest.

getting-started.title: Getting started
getting-started.lede: Add a workflow file, point it at your Azure AI Translator credentials, and the action will scan the repo for source-language resource files and emit translated siblings.

provision.title: Provision an Azure AI Translator resource
provision.body: Create an Azure AI Translator resource in the Azure portal, then store the following as GitHub repository secrets so the workflow can read them:
provision.key: the subscription key for the resource.

resource.title: Commit a source resource file
resource.body: Resource files use the {namingConvention} convention. For example {sourceFile} becomes {frFile}, {deFile}, and so on after a run.

output.title: Inspect the output
output.body: Once the workflow finishes, the action exposes three outputs you can wire into a PR description: {summaryTitle}, {summaryDetails}, and {hasNewTranslations}. It also writes a Markdown summary into the job page via {coreSummary}.

nextSteps.title: Next steps
nextSteps.recipes: See recipes for monorepos, custom glossaries, and Custom Translator categories.

providers.title: Translation providers
providers.lede: Choose one translation provider per run — Azure AI Translator, AWS Translate, or Google Cloud Translation — behind a single, unified API surface. The translator behaves identically regardless of provider; only the credentials differ.
providers.backwardCompatible: Fully backward compatible: {provider} defaults to {azure}, so existing Azure workflows keep working unchanged — no edits required.

selecting.title: Selecting a provider
selecting.body: Set the {provider} input to {azure} (default), {aws}, or {google}, then supply that provider's credentials. Only one provider is used per action call; the action deterministically delegates to the matching SDK behind a factory.

aws.name: AWS Translate
aws.credentials: OIDC / default chain, or awsAccessKeyId + awsSecretAccessKey; awsRegion

azure.title: Azure AI Translator (default)
azure.body: The default provider. Provision an Azure AI Translator resource and pass {subscriptionKey}, {endpoint}, and optionally {region}. This is the same configuration existing workflows already use.

aws.title: AWS Translate
aws.body: Uses {sdk}. Prefer OIDC via {configureAwsCredentials} so no long-lived secrets are stored — the action reads the AWS SDK default credential chain. Alternatively, pass {awsAccessKeyId} and {awsSecretAccessKey} explicitly. A region is always required, via {awsRegion} or the {awsRegionEnv} environment variable.
aws.keysNote: To use static keys instead of OIDC, drop the {configureAwsCredentials} step and pass {awsAccessKeyId} / {awsSecretAccessKey} (via {secrets}) plus {awsRegion}.

google.title: Google Cloud Translation
google.body: Uses {sdk} (v2). Authenticate with either an API key ({googleApiKey}) or a service-account JSON credential ({googleCredentials}). Supply exactly one; an optional {googleProjectId} is inferred from the credential when omitted.

mapping.title: Intent-specifier mapping
mapping.body: Provider-specific specifiers are mapped where an equivalent exists. Everything else falls back to sensible defaults so behavior stays consistent.
mapping.caption: Intent-specifier mapping across providers

locales.title: Locale codes differ per provider
locales.body: Each provider uses its own locale codes (for example Simplified Chinese is {azureCode} on Azure, {awsCode} on AWS, and {googleCode} on Google). Codes pass through as-is and drive the output file names, so choose {toLocales} values your selected provider supports.

configuration.title: Configuration
configuration.lede: Express defaults once in the repository config file and let every workflow inherit them. Action inputs always win when both are provided.
configuration.intro: Override the path with the {configPath} input if you need a non-default location.

fields.sourceLocale: Falls back to the {sourceLocale} input if not set. Also forwarded to Translator as {fromLocale} so short strings aren't autodetected.
fields.toLocales: Either an array or a comma-separated string.
fields.includeExclude: Glob patterns evaluated against absolute and workspace-relative paths.
fields.categoryId: Azure {customTranslatorLink} category — your industry/domain knob (legal, medical, marketing tone, etc.). Sent as {categoryQuery}.
fields.textType: Defaults to {plain}. Use {html} only if your resource values contain real HTML fragments.
fields.profanityAction: How to treat profane terms in translated output.
fields.allowFallback: Set to {false} to fail the translation when your {categoryId} has no deployment for a target locale instead of falling back to the general model.
fields.apiVersion: Translator REST API version. Defaults to {version}.
fields.maxRetries: Maximum retry attempts on transient HTTP responses ({statusCodes}). Defaults to {defaultRetries}. Total HTTP calls per request is {totalCalls}.
fields.retryBackoffMs: Cap (ms) on any single backoff sleep. Defaults to {defaultBackoff}. Azure's {retryAfter} header is honored exactly when present; otherwise jittered exponential backoff is used, capped at this value.
fields.protectPlaceholders: Defaults to {true}. When on, tokens like {mustache}, {jsVar}, {indexZero}, {numberFormat}, {printf}, {positionalPrintf} and HTML entities are replaced with sentinel tokens before translation and restored afterward. Disable only when source intentionally contains placeholder-shaped literals.
fields.customPlaceholderPatterns: Extra regex patterns (without delimiters) added to the default placeholder set, e.g. {angleToken} for custom token syntax. Invalid regexes are ignored.
fields.noTranslatePatterns: Glob patterns matched against parser-level keys (JSON dotted path, RESX {resxName}, PO {poMsgid}, XLIFF unit {xliffId}, INI/restext key). Matching keys are dropped from the request and pass through with their source value preserved.

steps.actionInputs: The action reads explicit inputs from the workflow.
steps.config: It loads the YAML config from {configPath}.
steps.precedence: For every shared field, the input wins; otherwise the YAML value is used.
steps.validation: The merged values are validated. Invalid enums (e.g. {invalidEnum}) cause the action to fail fast with a clear message.

tone.heading: Tone and industry, in plain terms
tone.body: The Azure AI Translator v3 API does not have a single tone knob. Two settings combine to give you most of the same control:
tone.industryBody: use {categoryId} to point at an Azure {customTranslatorLink} category trained on your own corpus — legal, technical, medical, marketing, or anything else. This is the strongest lever for tone, brand voice, and vocabulary.
tone.surfaceBody: use the {glossaryLink} to lock brand names and product terms verbatim, and use {profanityAction} to scrub profane terms when targeting localization for younger audiences.

inputs.title: Inputs & outputs
inputs.lede: Every input is also accepted in the optional repo config file. When both are set, the action input wins.
inputs.configurationIntro: See {configurationLink} for the YAML schema and loading order.

items.sourceLocale: Source locale (e.g. {en}) used to find resource files such as {sourceGlob}. Also forwarded to the provider as {fromLocale}.

providerInputs.heading: Provider selection & credentials
providerInputs.caption: Provider selection and per-vendor credentials
providerInputs.intro: Choose one provider per run with {provider}. Supply only the selected provider's credentials — see the {providersLink} guide for full setup.

items.provider: Translation provider: {azure} (default), {aws}, or {google}. Only one provider is used per action call.
items.subscriptionKey: Azure AI Translator subscription key. Required for {azure}. Pass via {secrets}.
items.endpoint: Azure Translator endpoint URL, e.g. {endpointUrl}. Required for {azure}.
items.awsAccessKeyId: AWS access key id. Optional — omit (with {awsSecretAccessKey}) to use the AWS default credential chain / OIDC. Pass via {secrets}.
items.awsSecretAccessKey: AWS secret access key. Must be supplied together with {awsAccessKeyId}. Pass via {secrets}.
items.awsSessionToken: Optional AWS session token for temporary credentials. Pass via {secrets}.
items.awsRegion: AWS region for the Translate service (e.g. {regionExample}). Required for {aws} unless {awsRegionEnv} is set in the environment.
items.googleApiKey: Google Cloud API key. Provide this OR {googleCredentials} for {google}. Pass via {secrets}.
items.googleCredentials: Google Cloud service-account key as a JSON string. Provide this OR {googleApiKey} for {google}. Pass via {secrets}.
items.googleProjectId: Optional Google Cloud project id. Usually inferred from the service-account credential.

items.region: Translator resource region (omit for global resources).
items.toLocales: Locales to translate to. Accepts a JSON array ({jsonArray}) or comma-separated values.
items.include: Newline-separated glob patterns; only matching files are translated.
items.exclude: Newline-separated glob patterns; matching files are skipped (applied after include).
items.configPath: Path to the YAML config file (relative to the workspace root).
items.dryRun: When {true}, run translations and emit summaries but do not write files.
items.failOnError: When {true}, unexpected errors fail the action; when {false}, errors are warnings.

translatorInputs.heading: Azure Translator request inputs
translatorInputs.intro: These map directly onto Azure AI Translator v3 query parameters and apply to the azure provider. textType and profanityAction are also mapped to AWS/Google where an equivalent exists (see the providers guide); the rest are Azure-only. Every one is optional; the Translator defaults apply when unset.
translatorInputs.caption: Azure Translator request inputs

items.categoryId: Azure {customTranslatorLink} category — the industry/domain/tone knob. Forwarded as {categoryQuery}.
items.textType: {plain} or {html}. Use {html} only when your resource values are real HTML fragments.
items.profanityAction: {noAction}, {marked}, or {deleted}. Controls how profane terms are emitted in translations.
items.allowFallback: When {false}, Translator returns an error instead of falling back to the general system if your {categoryId} has no deployment for a target locale.

resilienceInputs.heading: Resilience & placeholder protection
resilienceInputs.intro: These inputs control how the action behaves when Translator throttles your request and how it shields placeholder tokens like {mustache} or {indexZero} from being mangled during translation.
resilienceInputs.caption: Resilience and placeholder protection inputs

items.maxRetries: Maximum retry attempts on transient HTTP responses ({statusCodes}). The total number of HTTP calls per request is {totalCalls}.
items.retryBackoffMs: Cap (in milliseconds) on any single backoff sleep. The action honors Azure's {retryAfter} response header exactly when present; otherwise it uses jittered exponential backoff capped at this value.
items.protectPlaceholders: When {true} (default), placeholders such as {mustache}, {jsVar}, {indexZero}, {numberFormat}, {printf}, {positionalPrintf} and HTML entities are replaced with sentinel tokens before translation and restored afterward. Disable only when your source intentionally contains literal placeholder-shaped text that should be translated.
items.customPlaceholderPatterns: Newline-separated regexes (without delimiters) added to the default placeholder set, e.g. {angleToken} for custom token syntax.
items.noTranslatePatterns: Newline-separated glob patterns matched against parser-level keys (JSON dotted path, RESX {resxName}, PO {poMsgid}, XLIFF unit {xliffId}, INI/restext key). Matching keys are dropped from the Translator request and pass through with their source value preserved.

items.summaryDetails: Markdown summary suitable for a PR body or job summary.
items.hasNewTranslations: {trueString} when one or more new translations were generated.

formats.title: File formats
formats.lede: Six round-trippable resource formats. The action reads each source-locale file, translates the values, and writes a sibling per target locale.
formats.namingConvention: All files use the {namingConvention} naming convention.

resx.blurb: XML-based .NET resource files. Translates the inner text of each {dataValue} node, preserving comments and metadata.

xliff.blurb: OASIS XLIFF 2.0 documents. Translates each {source} and writes the result into the corresponding {target}, setting {trgLang} for the locale.

po.blurb: GNU gettext Portable Object files. Translates each {msgid} / {msgidPlural} into {msgstr} / {msgstrPlural} forms.

json.blurb: Arbitrary JSON files. Nested objects are flattened with the {delimiter} delimiter for translation, then re-nested on output. Dotted keys ({dottedKey}) are preserved verbatim.

ini.blurb: Simple {keyValue} files (Win32-style INI or any flat config). Section headers and comments are preserved.

restext.blurb: Microsoft MUI text resources — flat {keyValue} pairs used as a textual companion to {resx}.

roundTrip.heading: Round-trip guarantees
roundTrip.body: The test suite verifies byte-for-byte round-tripping for representative fixtures of every format. If a format quirk breaks round-tripping in your codebase, please {issueLink} with a minimal sample.

recipes.title: Recipes
recipes.lede: Common patterns for shipping translations: monorepos, glossaries, custom categories, dry runs, and tone control.

pullRequest.heading: Open a pull request after each run

glossary.heading: Glossary for brand names and product terms
glossary.body: Glossary terms are applied {after} translation, so the machine-translated output keeps your preferred branding even when the model swapped it for a synonym.

customCategory.heading: Custom Translator category (industry / domain)
customCategory.body: Train an Azure {customTranslatorLink} category on a corpus that captures your industry vocabulary and tone (legal, medical, marketing, internal-engineering, etc.) and pass its ID via {categoryId}. This is the strongest tone lever Azure offers.

tone.heading: Tone control + strict fallback
tone.body: Combine a Custom Translator {categoryId} with {profanityAction} and {allowFallbackFalse} to enforce a consistent voice and fail the workflow if the chosen category does not have a deployment for one of your target locales.

dryRun.heading: Dry run before turning the workflow on
dryRun.body: The action still emits summary outputs and a step summary, but no files are written. Combine with {failOnErrorFalse} to surface every issue as a warning while you iterate.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.lede: Quick answers to the questions that come up most often when adopting the action.

azure.heading: How does the action talk to Azure?
azure.body: Through Microsoft's official {azureSdkLink} SDK, layered on {corePipelineLink}. That gets you automatic retries on {statusCodes}, typed request and response models, and authentication header handling for free — everything that used to be a hand-rolled {axios} call. The public action surface (inputs, outputs, file outputs) is unchanged.

tone.heading: Why are translations sometimes off-brand or off-tone?
tone.body: Machine translation defaults to a general-purpose model. Three knobs help: a {glossaryLink} for hard term overrides, a {customTranslatorLink} for fine-tuned models trained on your tone and vocabulary, and {profanityAction} to scrub or flag profane terms.

duplicateFiles.heading: How do I avoid translating the same file twice?
duplicateFiles.body: Trigger the workflow on {paths} that match your source-locale files only, e.g. {sourceGlob}. The action also includes its own per-trigger file filter when {githubToken} is set, narrowing work to files in the latest commit.

offline.heading: Can I run the action offline?
offline.body: Not exactly — Azure AI Translator is the runtime brain. But you can run with {dryRunTrue} to validate parsing and config without making any HTTP requests for translation.

node.heading: What Node version does the action run on?
node.body: v3 runs on the GitHub Actions {nodeRuntime} runtime. The bundled JavaScript is compatible with Node 20, 22, and 24, which is what CI tests.

dist.heading: Where does the bundled {distDir} come from?
dist.body: GitHub JS actions ship their compiled JS in the repository. The {distBuild} workflow rebuilds it after every merge to {mainBranch} if it would drift, and {distCheck} blocks PRs that would land out-of-date output.

formats.heading: What about other resource formats?
formats.body: Today: {resx}, {xliff}, {po}, {json}, {ini}, {restext}. {issueLink} if you need another — the parser interface is small (parse → map → apply translations → format) and contributions are welcome.

translations.title: Translations
translations.lede: This page is translated by Resource Translator itself, on every push to main, into every language Azure AI Translator supports.
translations.statusPending: {translatedCount} currently rendered — {pendingCount} pending the next {workflowName} run.
translations.filterPlaceholder: Search by language, code, or native name…

steps.source: The English source file lives at {messagesPath}.
steps.workflow: Pushes that touch it kick off {workflowLink}, which runs {thisAction} against itself.
steps.fanOut: The action fans out to every Azure AI Translator-supported locale (configured in {configPath}) and writes a sibling {messagesLocaleJson} per target.
steps.pullRequest: A pull request is opened with the new translations. Merging it republishes this page with the cards filled in.

translations.poweredBy: Powered by Resource Translator and Azure AI Translator.

contributing.title: Contributing
contributing.lede: Bug reports, feature requests, and pull requests are all welcome. The action is small enough to be friendly to first-time contributors.

steps.extension: Add the file extension to {translationFileSchemes} in {finderFile}.
steps.tests: Write round-trip tests for parse → format and applyTranslations.

coverage.heading: Coverage thresholds
coverage.body: The Jest config enforces minimum coverage. New code must keep the statements + lines threshold above 78% and functions above 90%. Add fixtures and tests alongside your change.

docsSite.heading: Documentation site
docsSite.body: The {docsDir} folder is an Astro 6 + Tailwind v4 project. Run {npmInstall} there once, then {npmRunDev} for local preview. The {pagesWorkflow} workflow ships docs to GitHub Pages on every merge.

changelog.title: Changelog
changelog.lede: High-level summary of releases. The canonical, authoritative changelog lives in CHANGELOG.md in the repository.

releases.v302: {label} Translator requests now pin {apiVersion} on the wire for both {translate} and {languages}. The Azure SDK only honors the client-level api-version option when a credential is passed; the action authenticates per request via headers, so that option was silently dropped and requests defaulted to a preview api-version whose {translate} contract rejected the body with HTTP 400 (error code {code}). Completes the fix started in 3.0.1.
releases.v301: {label} The {translate} request body is now a bare JSON array of {textItem} items instead of a wrapped {inputs} object, matching the v3.0 REST contract. On its own this did not restore translations — see 3.0.2.

items.newInputs: New inputs: {include}, {exclude}, {configPath}, {categoryId}, {apiVersion}, {dryRun}, {failOnError}, plus advanced Translator knobs {textType}, {profanityAction}, {profanityMarker}, and {allowFallback}.
items.sourceLocale: Source locale is now also forwarded to Translator as {fromLocale} so short strings aren't autodetected.
items.resilience: {label} automatic retry on transient HTTP responses ({statusCodes}) honoring Azure's {retryAfter} header — falls back to jittered exponential backoff when absent. Tunable via {maxRetries} and {retryBackoffMs}. {issue}
items.placeholderProtection: {label} tokens like {mustache}, {jsVar}, {indexZero}, {numberFormat}, {printf}, {positionalPrintf} and HTML entities are wrapped in sentinels before translation and restored afterward, so Translator stops mangling i18next/Mustache/Handlebars/.NET/printf placeholders. Toggle via {protectPlaceholders}; extend with {customPlaceholderPatterns}. {issue}
items.perKeyOptOut: {label} new {noTranslatePatterns} input drops matching keys from the Translator request entirely (preserves source values for brand names, error codes, etc.). Matches glob patterns against parser-level keys (JSON dotted path, RESX {resxName}, PO {poMsgid}, XLIFF unit {xliffId}, INI/restext key). {issue}
items.tooling: esbuild bundler, ESLint 9 flat config, Prettier, EditorConfig.
items.docsSite: Astro + Tailwind v4 docs site with view transitions and Expressive Code.
items.docsAccessibility: {label} WCAG 2.1 AA pass. Logo glyphs converted to paths so they no longer fail color-contrast against gradient stops, the floating {backToTop} button uses {inert} while hidden (instead of an {ariaHidden} toggle that left it tabbable), {formatsAstro} sections no longer collide with their auto-generated heading IDs, three locale cards now expose BCP-47-valid {lang} attributes ({nya}, {lug}, {run}), duplicate {documentation} landmark labels are disambiguated between sidebar and footer, and every data table on {inputs}/{configuration} ships with a screen-reader caption.
items.inlineCode: {label} docs prose {codeElement} now wears the warm/amber theme accent — distinct from the brand→accent palette used for links and headings.
items.automation: Dependabot auto-merge, CodeQL scanning, CI matrix.
